name: Pinga
description: Pinga is a significant figure in Inuit mythology, primarily known as a goddess associated with hunting, fertility, and healing. She is often considered the guardian of animals, particularly caribou, and plays a crucial role in ensuring successful hunts for the Inuit people. Pinga's power extends to the realm of the dead, where she is believed to guide souls to the afterlife, offering comfort and protection on their journey. Her influence over fertility makes her a vital deity for the continuation of life and the health of women and children. Pinga's healing abilities are invoked in times of sickness and injury, highlighting her benevolent nature and her dedication to the well-being of the Inuit community. She is revered for her connection to the natural world and her ability to maintain balance between humans and the environment. Pinga's stories often emphasize the importance of respect for animals and the need to live in harmony with nature to ensure survival and prosperity. Offerings and prayers are made to her to secure favorable hunting conditions, healthy offspring, and protection from harm. Her role as a protector and provider makes her a central figure in Inuit spiritual life, embodying the values of sustenance, nurturing, and respect for the delicate balance of the ecosystem.
subtitle: Goddess of Hunting, Fertility, and Healing

keyMyths.title: Pinga and the Caribou Herd
keyMyths.description: Pinga guides the caribou herds to the hunters, ensuring they have sustenance. Respect for the animals is key to continued success.

keyMyths.title: Pinga's Healing Touch
keyMyths.description: A sick child is healed by Pinga after prayers and offerings are made. This demonstrates her power over health and well-being.

keyMyths.title: Pinga's Guidance to the Afterlife
keyMyths.description: Pinga escorts the souls of the deceased to the next world, providing comfort and protection. She ensures their safe passage.

sources.author: Franz Boas
